mcqs.question: What is the main difference between a structure and a union in C?

options.text: structure allocates separate memory for each member, while a union shares memory among all members
union can hold more data types than a structure
structure is used for integers only, while a union is used for all data types
There is no difference between structures and unions

mcqs.explanation: The key difference is that structures allocate separate memory for each member, while unions share the same memory location for all members.

mcqs.question: What is the size of the following union? union Example { int a; float b; char c[10]; };

options.text: 4 bytes
10 bytes
14 bytes
Size of the largest member (10 bytes)

mcqs.explanation: The size of a union is equal to the size of its largest member. Here, the char array c[10] is the largest member (10 bytes).

mcqs.question: How do you access a member of a structure in C?

options.text: Using the dot operator (.)
Using the arrow operator (->) if accessing through a pointer
Both A and B
Using the colon operator (:)

mcqs.explanation: Structure members can be accessed using the dot operator (.) for direct access or the arrow operator (->) when using a pointer to the structure.

mcqs.question: What happens when you modify a member of a union in C?

options.text: All other members remain unchanged
All other members are automatically updated to the same value
The values of other members become undefined
The union is automatically resized

mcqs.explanation: Since all members of a union share the same memory location, modifying one member will corrupt the values of other members, making them undefined.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is true about structures in C?

options.text: Structures cannot contain other structures as members
The size of a structure is always equal to the sum of its members' sizes
Structures can be passed to functions by value or by reference
Structures cannot have pointers as members

mcqs.explanation: Structures can be passed to functions either by value (copy) or by reference (pointer). The size may include padding for alignment.
